# ARTICLE 5: THE SHAKES ARE THE ALARM WAKING UP

> Withdrawal is re-awakening, not punishment.

When I was deep in addiction, the worst part wasn't the using. It was the stopping. Every time I tried to quit, my body betrayed me. The shakes started first – a fine tremor that made my hands useless. Then the sweating. Then the heart pounding in my chest like a trapped bird. Then the panic – the overwhelming sense that something terrible was about to happen, that I was going to die, that I couldn't survive another minute without a pill or a pipe.

I believed the shakes were proof of my weakness. Proof that I was an addict. Proof that I deserved the shame. I was wrong.

The shakes aren't the drug leaving your body as punishment. They're your alarm waking back up after being muzzled for years.

## The Myth of Withdrawal as Moral Reckoning

Society treats withdrawal like a reckoning. The suffering is supposed to teach you a lesson. "That's what you get for using." "Pay the piper." "Your body is punishing you for your choices." This narrative is everywhere. In twelve-step rooms. In court-ordered treatment. In the way families talk about the addict who's trying to get clean. But it's not science. And it's not helping.

Withdrawal is not a punishment. It's a predictable physiological event. None of the experts would argue with that – the brain adapts to the presence of a substance, and when you remove it abruptly, the system overshoots. It goes into a state of hyperarousal that can feel like you're crawling out of your own skin. That's not justice. That's physics.

## What's Actually Happening Inside

Here's the simplified version. When you take opioids, alcohol, benzodiazepines, or stimulants regularly, your brain adjusts. It compensates for the drug's effects to try to maintain balance. Alcohol and benzos, for example, work on the brain's main brake pedal. Chronic use causes your brain to turn down its own brake production. When you stop, there's nothing left to calm the excitatory gas pedal. The brain is literally on fire with excitation.

The shakes, the sweats, the racing heart – those are the excitatory systems running unchecked. They're not a message from the universe about your moral worth. They're a message from your brainstem: "We have lost our balance. We need time to find it again."

I didn't know the neurochemistry back then. I just knew my body was screaming. The experts have a term for what happens to the brain during chronic use – neuroadaptation, the building of a chemical scaffolding just to stay upright. When you take the scaffolding away, the structure doesn't stand on its own. It collapses. And the collapse hurts.

## The Alarm Was Already Blaring Before the First Use

Here's what nobody told me when I was trying to get clean. The alarm was already going off before I ever took a pill. My baseline was never calm. It was panic. From the NICU – three pounds, four ounces, wires and alarms, a priest giving me last rites – my nervous system learned that danger was normal. From the blanket at six, frozen while my mother was beaten, I learned that stillness equals survival.

By the time I found opiates at seventeen, my alarm had been blaring for years. The pills didn't create the noise. They temporarily silenced it. So when I tried to quit, the alarm didn't just come back. It came back furious. It had been suppressed – not healed, not retrained, just muzzled. And now it was free, and it was louder than ever.

The shakes aren't the drug leaving. They're the alarm waking up from a chemically induced coma.

## The Shame Spiral

Here's where the system fails. A person in withdrawal is already in a state of extreme physiological distress. They're shaking, sweating, unable to sleep. Their nervous system is in emergency mode. Now add shame. "You did this to yourself." "This is what you deserve." "If you were stronger, this wouldn't happen." That shame doesn't motivate healing. It triggers the freeze response. It makes the person want to disappear – or to use again just to make the suffering stop.

I've watched friends die in that spiral. They'd try to quit. The withdrawal would hit. The shame would hit harder. They'd use again – not to get high, but to stop the screaming. And then they'd hate themselves more. You can't shame someone out of a physiological state. You can only make it worse.

## What Helped Me Stop Running

I didn't get clean through willpower. I got clean when I stopped fighting my nervous system and started listening to it. I learned that the shakes were data. Not weakness. Not punishment. Just data. My nervous system was telling me: "We have been out of balance for a long time. We need help finding our way back."

That shift – from "I'm being punished" to "My system is re-calibrating" – changed everything. I stopped white-knuckling through withdrawal and started supporting my nervous system. Hydration. Rest. Simple grounding. And most importantly, self-compassion. Instead of screaming at myself for being weak, I said: "Of course you're shaking. You've been suppressing this alarm for twenty years. It's going to take time."

I still remember the first time I sat through a withdrawal without using. My hands were on the kitchen table, vibrating with tremors, sweat running down my face. And instead of calling myself a failure, I whispered: "That's my alarm waking up. It's been waiting a long time to be heard." The shakes didn't stop. But the fear did.

The storm wired us for danger. The shakes aren't punishment – they're my alarm waking up. And waking up hurts.
